summary: Add a read-only OPNsense mirror integration cloned from the Proxmox precedent end-to-end. New OPNsenseRouter target model (host/port/verify_tls/ca_bundle_pem + Fernet-encrypted api_key/api_secret, ipam_space_id RESTRICT, optional dns_group_id, mirror flags, sync_interval), an httpx async client hitting the four REST endpoints + ARP, a per-router reconciler that mirrors LAN/VLAN/OPT* interfaces -> Subnets, DHCPv4 leases -> IPAddress status="dhcp", static reservations -> status="reserved", and ARP table -> status="opnsense-arp" (secondary), a 30s beat sweep with per-router interval gating gated on PlatformSettings.integration_opnsense_enabled, full CRUD/test/sync router, and complete shared-file wiring across feature-module gating, both dashboard surfaces (NN #15), MCP tools, sidebar, and routing.

(4) add platform_settings.integration_opnsense_enabled Boolean NOT NULL server_default false. (5) Seed feature_module row id='integrations.opnsense' enabled=false (mirror the INSERT ... open_questions: api_key storage: OPNsense API 'key' is an account identifier (not strictly secret) and 'secret' is the secret — spec stores api_key plaintext (like proxmox token_id) and api_secret Fernet-encrypted. Confirm the integrator is comfortable with key-plaintext, or encrypt both in one combined Fernet blob if security review prefers.
Default port: OPNsense web/API is HTTPS on 443 by default (spec uses 443); some operators run the GUI on a custom port. Default 443 chosen — confirm vs 8443.

Interface->Subnet selection: getInterfaceConfig returns ALL interfaces (incl. WAN, loopback, GIFs). Spec mirrors LAN/VLAN/OPT* only (per issue) and skips WAN/public/unaddressed — confirm whether WAN's public IP should also land as a 'reserved' gateway row.

ARP as IP rows: ARP entries are L2 neighbor sightings, not allocations. Spec uses a dedicated status='opnsense-arp' (added to IP_STATUSES_INTEGRATION_OWNED) gated behind mirror_arp (default off) so it never collides with dhcp/reserved rows — confirm this status name vs reusing 'discovered'.

Lease ownership vs Kea: status='dhcp' rows are also produced by the native Kea mirror (auto_from_lease). OPNsense rows are distinguished by opnsense_router_id FK; the dhcp_lease_cleanup sweep keys on auto_from_lease — confirm setting auto_from_lease=True on OPNsense rows won't cause the Kea expiry sweep to prematurely delete them (the reconciler re-creates each pass, so acceptable, but flag for integrator).
DHCPv4 only per the issue endpoints — DHCPv6/Kea-on-OPNsense not in scope for this issue.

summary: Add a first-class nullable `decom_date` (SQLAlchemy `Date`, indexed) to both `Subnet` and `IPAddress`, threaded through the IPAM Pydantic create/update/response schemas. Add a new `decom_expiring` alert rule type that reuses `threshold_days` (NO alerts migration — same pattern as the just-merged `secret_expiring` #76 and `service_term_expiring`), with a matcher that scans non-soft-deleted subnets whose `decom_date` falls within the window, plus an admin Dashboard widget (client-side aggregation over the already-fetched subnet list, mirroring `DomainsSummaryCard`). The existing `alerts-evaluate` beat task (every 60 s) is the "generates a 30-day summary feeding alerts" mechanism — no new beat entry needed; the issue's "subnets decom in next 30 days summary" is the alert events opened by the new rule plus the dashboard widget rollup.

Mirror the Date-column precedent in alembic/versions/e1d8c92a4f73_network_service_catalog.py (term_end_date: sa.Column('term_end_date', sa.Date(), nullable=True) + op.create_index('ix_network_service_term_end_date', ...)). NO column for the alert rule itself — decom_expiring reuses the existing alert_rule.threshold_days, exactly like secret_expiring/service_term_expiring. summary: Add a global, system-wide read-only "maintenance mode" driven by three new PlatformSettings columns (maintenance_mode_enabled / maintenance_message / maintenance_started_at). Enforce via a new Starlette BaseHTTPMiddleware (app.core.maintenance_mode) registered in main.create_app() that 503s every mutating request (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) with a Retry-After header and a structured body — with a superadmin bypass (decode the bearer in-middleware, check is_effective_superadmin) and an exempt-path allowlist (auth, the settings toggle, agent long-polls/heartbeats). Surface the flag through /health/platform, render a new global top banner (distinct from the existing appliance host-maintenance banner), add a Security-group toggle on SettingsPage, audit the toggle (#4), and register read + write MCP tools (#13).

backend_new_files: backend/app/core/maintenance_mode.py :: MaintenanceModeMiddleware (Starlette BaseHTTPMiddleware) + helpers. Holds a process-local short-TTL cache of (enabled, message, started_at) read from platform_settings (mirror the feature_modules.py monotonic + _CACHE_TTL_S=5.0 + invalidate_cache() pattern). dispatch(): if request.method in {POST,PUT,PATCH,DELETE} and path not in EXEMPT_PREFIXES and maintenance enabled -> attempt superadmin bypass (decode bearer via app.core.security.decode_access_token / _resolve_api_token-style lookup; reuse app.api.deps logic minimally, fall back to NO bypass on any decode failure) -> if not effective superadmin return JSONResponse(503, {detail, maintenance: true, message, started_at}, headers={'Retry-After': '120'}). Export is_maintenance_mode(db)/get_maintenance_state(db) + invalidate_cache() for the settings router to call after a toggle. EXEMPT_PREFIXES must include /api/v1/auth (login/refresh/logout), /api/v1/settings (so superadmin can toggle it off — though superadmin bypass already covers this, keep belt-and-suspenders), /health, /metrics, and agent endpoints that POST heartbeats/long-polls (/api/v1/dns/agents, /api/v1/dhcp/agents, /api/v1/appliance/supervisor) so a maintenance window doesn't break agent config caching (non-negotiable #5).

summary: Add a `time_bound_grant` table (group_id, action, resource_type, resource_id, expires_at, granted_by, reason, revoked_at) that is consulted live by `user_has_permission` as an additive union over the static role grants. Live grants are loaded once per request in `app.api.deps` (both the JWT path `get_current_user` and the API-token path `_resolve_api_token`) and stashed on the User object so the synchronous `user_has_permission` can walk them without an async call. A Celery beat sweep (run_every=60s) deactivates rows whose `expires_at` has passed, writing one `audit_log` row per revoked grant attributed to the synthetic `system` user. CRUD lives on the existing `/groups` surface as a sub-router; the Groups admin page gets a per-group "Time-bound access" panel plus a create-grant modal with an expiry picker. MCP gets `find_time_bound_grants` (read, default-enabled) and `propose_grant_temporary_access` (write proposal, default-disabled).

migration.detail: New table `time_bound_grant`: id UUID PK (default uuid4); group_id UUID FK -> group.id ON DELETE CASCADE, NOT NULL, indexed; action String(50) NOT NULL; resource_type String(100) NOT NULL; resource_id String(255) NULL (None/empty = whole-type, mirroring Role.permissions resource_id semantics in core/permissions._resource_id_matches); expires_at DateTime(timezone=True) NOT NULL, indexed (the sweep filters on it); revoked_at DateTime(timezone=True) NULL (NULL = live; non-NULL = swept/revoked — keep the row as an audit breadcrumb rather than hard-delete); reason String(1000) NOT NULL default ''; granted_by_user_id UUID FK -> user.id ON DELETE SET NULL, NULL; created_at/modified_at via TimestampMixin (server_default now()). Composite index ix_time_bound_grant_live on (group_id, revoked_at, expires_at) for the per-request load filter `group_id IN (...) AND revoked_at IS NULL AND expires_at > now()`. Chain `down_revision` off the single alembic head at merge time (currently three open heads — d6e1a4b9f082 / c5f2a9b18e34 / c7a3e1f90d24; integrator picks whichever is the linear head after the prior 8 features merge). NO platform_settings column change is required — sweep cadence is fixed at 60s; if an opt-out toggle is wanted, add a `time_bound_grant_sweep_enabled` Boolean to platform_settings, but default behavior needs none.

Edit lands on the existing PUT /api/v1/settings (the snmp_/ntp_ precedent flows through the single settings endpoint, NOT a dedicated /settings/snmp route), with the ca_cert_pem TLS secret Fernet-encrypted exactly like snmp_community. Frontend adds a Syslog view to FleetTab's Services nav group plus a Fleet status chip (forwarding/unreachable/disabled) driven by a new appliance.syslog_forwarding heartbeat status column. open_questions: Which host user owns authorized_keys: issue says ~admin/.ssh/authorized_keys — confirm the appliance's admin account name (the firewall_renderer/console wizard may reference a specific user; if there are multiple login users, render needs AllowUsers/Match scaffolding to scope keys). Need the canonical appliance admin username.

Is ssh_key_count for the Fleet chip per-host (supervisor reports actual applied authorized_keys line count via a status sidecar, like snmpd_running) or global (just len(platform_settings.ssh_authorized_keys))? Spec assumes per-host applied count (mirrors snmpd_running/ntp_sync_state precedent) which proves the keys were actually written; confirm vs. simpler global count.

Privileged-port handling: issue says 'refuse ssh_port already in use on a privileged port' — clarify whether the server-side check should reject ANY privileged port (<1024) other than 22, or whether 'already in use' detection is purely a host-runner concern (the host can't know port usage at PUT time). Spec proposes: server rejects <1024 except 22; host runner does the real bind/in-use check + refuses+logs.

PermitRootLogin granularity: issue gives ssh_allow_root_login bool — map false→'no' and true→'yes' (or 'prohibit-password' when password auth is off)? Confirm desired sshd directive value for the true case.

Should the SSH nft drop-in be source-scoped to ssh_allowed_source_networks (CIDR-restricted accept) or open ssh_port unconditionally with sshd's own Match/AllowUsers doing filtering? SNMP opens the port unscoped and lets snmpd filter; chrony opens unscoped and lets chrony 'allow' filter. sshd has no native source-CIDR filter, so the nft drop-in likely MUST be source-scoped here — confirm this divergence from the SNMP/NTP precedent.

summary: Add appliance DNS-resolver (systemd-resolved) host-config, mirroring the SNMP (#153) / NTP (#154) / LLDP (#343) host-config plane exactly. Add resolver_* columns to the platform_settings singleton, a render_resolved_conf()/resolver_bundle() service that emits an /etc/systemd/resolved.conf.d/spatiumddi.conf drop-in, fold the resolver_settings block into BOTH agent config surfaces (DHCP-agent /config long-poll and supervisor heartbeat), add a supervisor maybe_fire_resolver_reload() trigger writer + a host-side spatiumddi-resolved-reload runner/.path/.service, and ship a Resolver tab under Appliance with DoT + DNSSEC pickers. GET/PUT done via dedicated /settings/resolver endpoints (admin + audited) added inside the existing settings router, plus the resolver fields added to the shared SettingsResponse/SettingsUpdate + PlatformSettings TS type so the full read stays complete.

migration.detail: Add 6 columns to platform_settings (singleton): resolver_mode VARCHAR(16) NOT NULL server_default 'automatic'; resolver_servers JSONB NOT NULL server_default '[]'::jsonb; resolver_fallback_servers JSONB NOT NULL server_default '[]'::jsonb; resolver_search_domains JSONB NOT NULL server_default '[]'::jsonb; resolver_dnssec VARCHAR(16) NOT NULL server_default 'allow-downgrade'; resolver_dns_over_tls VARCHAR(16) NOT NULL server_default 'no'. New alembic revision chaining off the CURRENT head — note repo has two open heads right now (d6e1a4b9f082 and c7a3e1f90d24); the integrator must chain off whichever is head after the other 8 features merge, or this lands as a batched migration with #156/#157 as the issue recommends (one revision adding all of #156/#157/#158 columns together to avoid a 3-way platform_settings migration collision). Mirror the column-list style of backend/alembic/versions/c87a3f29d108_appliance_ntp_settings.py. Optional (parity, not required for v1): appliance.resolver_status VARCHAR(16) NULL for heartbeat read-back like snmpd_running/ntp_sync_state.

feature_module.rationale: Appliance host-config surfaces (NTP/SNMP/LLDP/timezone) are NOT behind feature_modules — they are always-available appliance services rendered into a tab under the Appliance page. Resolver mirrors that: no feature_module key. (The only appliance feature_module is appliance.firewall, which gates an entirely separate router; resolver has no equivalent gated router.) Gating is instead via the resolver_mode='automatic' default = inert: render produces no drop-in, runner removes any existing drop-in.

open_questions: systemd-resolved restart vs reload: issue says 'systemctl restart systemd-resolved'. A restart briefly drops the host resolver; confirm acceptable (BIND9 in a container is the authoritative :53 and is unaffected; resolved only serves the host's own outbound lookups). If a momentary blip is a concern, 'systemctl reload-or-restart' is safer.

DNSStubListener interaction: the new drop-in must NOT set DNSStubListener (existing no-stub-listener.conf owns it). Confirm the runner removes ONLY spatiumddi.conf on revert-to-automatic and leaves no-stub-listener.conf intact.

Per-link vs global override: resolved global DNS= can be overridden by per-link DNS pushed by NetworkManager (dns=systemd-resolved). For an override to actually win, v1 may need to also emit 'Domains=~.' (route-only default domain) so global servers take precedence over per-link DHCP-provided ones — decide whether to emit it automatically in override mode or just document the caveat.

Migration batching: issue recommends a single batched platform_settings migration for #156/#157/#158. Integrator must choose one combined revision vs three chained AND first resolve the current two-open-heads state (d6e1a4b9f082 / c7a3e1f90d24).
